question: Define a cut-set in a connected graph G. Provide a small example graph and identify one of its cut-sets.
expectedPoints: A cut-set is a set of edges whose removal increases the number of connected components of the graph.
The removal of a minimal set of edges (a cut-set) is sufficient to increase the number of components.
Example provided correctly, e.g., a graph with a bridge or a cycle where a specific edge set disconnects it.

question: Explain the relationship between a cut-set and a cut vertex. How does the existence of a cut vertex in a graph relate to the cut-sets?
expectedPoints: A cut vertex is a vertex whose removal increases the number of connected components.
The edges incident to a cut vertex often form, or are part of, a cut-set.
The removal of a cut vertex effectively removes all its incident edges, which can be a cut-set if it disconnects the graph.

question: Prove that in a tree, every edge is a cut-set. Furthermore, explain why a tree with n vertices has exactly (n-1) cut-sets, each consisting of a single edge.
expectedPoints: A tree is a connected graph with no cycles, meaning it is minimally connected.
Removing any single edge from a tree will disconnect it, creating two components, thus qualifying that edge as a cut-set.
Since a tree has exactly (n-1) edges and each is a cut-set, there are exactly (n-1) such minimal cut-sets.
